chapter: 2026-06 expansion: inference-time scaling, mechanistic interpretability, RAG, model merging

glossary.def: The probability that at least one of N independent samples is correct, 1 - (1-p)^N. Also called coverage: it is the ceiling on what any answer-selection method can deliver, since you cannot pick an answer that was never sampled.
Sampling several chains of thought and returning the most frequent final answer. It raises accuracy without any verifier because correct reasoning paths tend to agree while errors scatter.

A verifier (ORM) that scores only a solution's final answer with one scalar. Cheap to label, but blind to where a wrong solution went off the rails and foolable by a wrong chain that reaches a right-looking answer.

A verifier (PRM) that scores each reasoning step and aggregates them (often a product or minimum) into a chain score. Step-level supervision rewards valid reasoning over lucky answers and gives search a dense signal.

A decode-time control on test-time compute that makes a reasoning model think longer (suppress the end-of-thinking token, append a cue like 'Wait') or shorter (force the end token after a cap), with no retraining.
The o1 / DeepSeek-R1 paradigm in which a model is trained, usually by RL with verifiable rewards, to produce one extended reasoning trace that plans, self-checks, and backtracks before answering.

Compute spent at inference rather than training - extra samples, search width, or longer reasoning - to raise accuracy. The compute-optimal split between model size and test-time spend is difficulty-dependent.

When best-of-N or search, pushed to large budgets, selects samples that score well by exploiting a learned verifier's blind spots rather than by being correct, which can make accuracy hump-shaped in the budget.
Reverse-engineering the actual algorithm a trained network runs, expressed as human-auditable features and circuits, with claims confirmed by causal experiments rather than correlations.
The tendency of a single neuron to activate for several unrelated concepts at once, because features are stored along directions that cut across the neuron basis rather than aligning with it.
A network's strategy of representing more features than it has dimensions by storing them as near-orthogonal directions, tolerating small interference because features are sparse.

An overcomplete autoencoder (SAE) trained to re-express activations as a sparse combination of a learned dictionary; reconstruction loss plus an L1 penalty push its atoms toward monosemantic features.
Describing a feature or neuron that responds to exactly one human-recognizable concept - the property sparse autoencoders aim to recover from polysemantic activations.
A causal-tracing method that transplants an activation from a corrupted run into a clean run at one location and measures the output change, localizing where a behavior is computed.
Applying the model's final unembedding to intermediate-layer residual-stream states to read the prediction forming layer by layer; a fast but approximate probe of where a computation happens.
Adding or clamping a learned feature's decoder direction in the residual stream to amplify or suppress the associated concept; reliable steering is strong evidence the feature is causally real.
Grounding a model's output in documents fetched at query time (RAG), for freshness, citations, and a smaller working context than stuffing everything into the prompt.
A scale-free way (RRF) to merge several ranked retrieval lists by summing 1/(K + rank) of each item across lists, avoiding any need to calibrate incompatible score scales.

A reranking model that feeds query and candidate document jointly through a transformer so every query token attends to every document token; far more accurate than a bi-encoder but cannot be precomputed.
A retriever that embeds query and document separately into the same vector space, so document vectors can be indexed offline and compared by a single dot product.

Hypothetical Document Embeddings: a query transformation that embeds an LLM-generated hypothetical answer instead of the question, because a fabricated answer sits nearer real answers in embedding space than the bare query.
Sub-linear vector search (ANN) such as HNSW graphs or IVF-PQ clusters that trades a little recall for large speedups over exact kNN on million-scale corpora.
The empirical U-shaped position effect where models use information placed in the middle of a long context far less reliably than information at the start or end.
A RAG generation metric, also called faithfulness, asking whether every claim in the answer is entailed by the retrieved passages - distinct from whether the answer is relevant or correct.
Tokens repeated between adjacent retrieval chunks (stride = chunk size minus overlap) that buy boundary-spanning recall at a proportional index-storage cost.
The difference between a fine-tuned and its pretrained weights, a same-shape displacement in weight space pointing toward a learned task; added to a base it installs the skill, negated it removes it.
Editing model behavior by adding, subtracting, or forming analogies of task vectors with no further training; for example subtracting a toxicity task vector to detoxify a model.
A model made by averaging the weights of several fine-tunes of one base; it often beats the single best ingredient while costing one model at inference.

A merge that resolves interference in three steps: trim each task vector to its top-magnitude entries, elect a per-coordinate sign by summed magnitude, then take the disjoint mean of only the sign-agreeing tasks.
Drop-and-rescale: randomly zeroes a fraction p of a task vector's entries and rescales the survivors by 1/(1-p), exploiting the redundancy of fine-tuning deltas so merged vectors collide less.
The property that the straight line in weight space between two models fine-tuned from the same base stays at low loss (no barrier), which is what makes weight averaging viable.
The maximum loss along the interpolation path between two models minus the mean of the endpoint losses; near zero means a merge is safe, large means averaging lands on a high-loss ridge.

Relabeling a layer's hidden units (and permuting the next layer to match) yields an identical function with different weights, so independently trained models must be aligned before they can be merged.
